message.content:
Research IREE's matmul code generation for RISC-V with RVV (RISC-V Vector Extension). Focus on:

1. How IREE tiles matmul operations for RISC-V targets
2. What compilation flags and attributes affect vectorization (look for vlen, scalable vectors, etc.)
3. How IREE's dispatch region formation works for matmul
4. Any RISC-V specific tiling heuristics or strategies in IREE

Search the web for:
- IREE RISC-V RVV matmul code generation
- IREE linalg.matmul tiling strategies
- IREE scalable vector support
- IREE dispatch region formation for matmul

Look for documentation, GitHub issues/PRs, and technical discussions about IREE's approach to RISC-V vectorization.

A comparison of the RISC-V vector… | by Erik Engheim | Medium","url":"https://erik-engheim.medium.com/arm-vs-risc-v-vector-extensions-992f201f402f"},{"title":"[RISCV] Use experimental.vp.splat to splat specific vector length ele… · llvm/llvm-project@c45fc69","url":"https://github.com/llvm/llvm-project/commit/c45fc691a2a39318fc146ba1665a2fe2d9f43b2b"}]

Based on the search results, here's what I found about IREE's scalable vector support and RVV vector length:

## IREE Scalable Vector Support

IREE demonstrates that scalable data-tiling for SVE can be integrated into a real ML compiler, proving that vector-length-agnostic codegen is both practical and highly effective, enabling multi-× speedups without requiring any model or framework changes. The mechanisms for scalable SVE support naturally extend to architectures such as Arm SME and RISC-V RVV, positioning this work as a blueprint for scalable vector enablement across upcoming CPU architectures.

## RVV Vector Length Support in IREE

If the vector extension is enabled in iree-llvm-target-cpu-features, the RVV VLS (vector length specific) code will be generated with the vector length specified by the riscv-v-vector-bits-min and riscv-v-fixed-length-vector-lmul-max options.

## Technical Approach

IREE introduced scalable data-tiled layouts by rewriting linalg.matmul into linalg.mmt4d with inner tile sizes computed from SVE's runtime vector length (vscale). This same approach can be applied to RVV implementations.

The work demonstrates how IREE handles the challenge of vector-length-agnostic compilation, where code can adapt to different vector lengths at runtime rather than being compiled for a fixed size.

Based on the web search results, here's information about IREE linalg.matmul tiling strategies for RISC-V:

## Tiling Strategies

Register Tiling focuses on maximizing the utilization of the CPU's vector registers during matrix multiplication, and by carefully organizing data to fit into vector registers, register tiling helps minimize memory access and maximize computational throughput. Cache Tiling optimizes matrix multiplication by improving data locality, ensuring that data remains in the cache hierarchy for as long as possible, and efficient cache tiling reduces memory latency and improves the performance of the matmul operation by minimizing cache misses.

In IREE, the matmul operation is implemented using an outer product approach, with the output register tile size being [m, n], where m and n represent the number of elements in the output rows and columns, respectively.

## RISC-V Implementation

IREE modified the iree-codegen-materialize-device-encoding pass to enable the transformation of contraction ops into linalg.mmt4d and to perform VLEN-aware tiling for RISC-V64 target. For the RISC-V target, IREE has implemented mmt4d microkernels.

## Tile Size Selection

Choosing a smaller tile size than the optimal leads to underutilization of hardware registers, while using bigger tile sizes increases register pressure that causes register spills and reloads and degrades performance.

The search results indicate that IREE's RISC-V backend support for optimized matmul operations is still an active area of development, with ongoing work to improve tiling strategies and microkernel performance.

Based on the search results, here's what I found about IREE's RISC-V RVV matmul code generation and vectorization:

## IREE's Matmul Implementation

In IREE, the matmul operation is implemented using an outer product approach. The output register tile size is [m, n], where m and n represent the number of elements in the output rows and columns, respectively.

## Matmul Vectorization in IREE

The lowering path of matmul in OpenXLA/IREE is: linalg.matmul → vector.contract → vector.outerproduct. However, when they successfully unblocked the end-to-end path, they found the code-gen quality is not good as expected.

## Code Generation Challenges

The IREE project has encountered compilation time and code quality challenges with RVV matmul vectorization on RISC-V targets. After landing changes that lower quantized_matmul ops to matmul ops which kicks in matmul vectorization, it takes 13 minutes to compile a model. Additionally, after the vectorization path of the quantized conv1x1 op was enabled, the library size for the workload becomes way larger than before, with the resulting .o size going from 215KB to >1MB.

## Optimization Approaches

Some hot-spot operations in IREE will be offloaded to SKL (SiFive Kernel Library) in order to maximize performance from SiFive processors. Additionally, matmul-f32 performance achieved a 3.1x times improvement through compiler optimizations on RVV.

I've found search results related to IREE dispatch region formation, matmul, GPU, and reduction operations. Here's a summary of the key findings:

## Dispatch Region Formation

When creating dispatches, operations that perform some kind of reduction are identified as "root" operations - in a typical example, these are the MatMul and the sum reduction. For each root op, surrounding operations that could be merged into the same dispatch are found, and then each of these groups of ops with a single root are split off into individual functions, creating dispatches.

## GPU Optimization Considerations

Dispatch regions are formed by "casting a net" around operations using a fixed heuristic that must not know about any hardware features, and the introduction of packing/unpacking operations is delayed.

## Split-K and Reduction Handling

To get extra parallelism, the reduction dimension can be split between a parallel and reduction dimension and create a second dispatch doing the remaining reduction, with ops going through dispatch creation to fuse the linalg and first generic op into one dispatch and the following reduction along with consumers into a second dispatch region.

## GPU Debugging and Analysis

If the CPU is working properly, the same dispatch region formation can be used and diff against the CPU dispatches one by one to isolate GPU problems. For the same dispatch, there may be different CodeGen pipelines - for matmul there can be a simple SIMT pipeline or using tensor/matrix cores.

The search results cover topics related to dispatch region formation strategies, GPU optimization patterns for matmul operations, and how reduction operations are handled during the compilation process in IREE.

Links: [{"title":"Accelerating GenAI Workloads by Enabling RISC-V Microkernel Support in IREE","url":"https://arxiv.org/html/2508.14899"},{"title":"LLM Optimization and Deployment on SiFive RISC-V Intelligence Products","url":"https://www.sifive.com/blog/llm-optimization-and-deployment-on-sifive-intellig"},{"title":"VMVX mmt4d ukernel by bjacob · Pull Request #10239 · iree-org/iree","url":"https://github.com/iree-org/iree/pull/10239"},{"title":"Proper distribution-tiling for `mmt4d` · Issue #16410 · iree-org/iree","url":"https://github.com/iree-org/iree/issues/16410"},{"title":"[CPU] Enable DT for SVE for linalg.matmul · Issue #16162 · iree-org/iree","url":"https://github.com/iree-org/iree/issues/16162"},{"title":"RISC-V cross-compilation - IREE","url":"https://scottamain.github.io/iree/building-from-source/riscv/"},{"title":"[2508.14899] Accelerating GenAI Workloads by Enabling RISC-V Microkernel Support in IREE","url":"https://arxiv.org/abs/2508.14899"},{"title":"iree/docs/website/docs/community/blog/posts/mmt4d.md at main · iree-org/iree","url":"https://github.com/iree-org/iree/blob/main/docs/website/docs/community/blog/posts/mmt4d.md"},{"title":"RFC: Turn the data-tiling path on by default for CPU backends","url":"https://groups.google.com/g/iree-discuss/c/lpQzYXw1r6s"},{"title":"Antmicro · Co-developing RISC-V AI solutions using Vector Extensions in Renode with Kenning bare-metal runtime and IREE","url":"https://antmicro.com/blog/2023/05/kenning-bare-metal-iree-runtime-renode/"}]

Based on the search results, here's what I found about IREE, mmt4d, RISC-V, tiling, and vlen:

## Key Findings

For the RISC-V target, mmt4d microkernels have been implemented as part of IREE's compiler infrastructure. A pass was modified to enable the transformation of contraction ops into linalg.mmt4d and to perform VLEN-aware tiling for RISC-V64 target.

### What is mmt4d?

linalg.mmt4d implements matrix-multiplication-by-transposed-right-hand-side, which works with both the left and right-hand sides being row-major-tiled. A 2D tiled layout can be represented as a 4D layout—for example, tensor<2x2x2x2xf32> represents a 4x4 matrix made of 2x2 tiles, where the row-major layout makes each 2x2 tile contiguous and row-major.

### VLEN-Aware Tiling on RISC-V

The implementation performs dynamic tiling based on VLEN (vector length). When VLEN=1024 with float data type and [m,n]=[8,32], IREE generates code with a fixed output register tile size, but this configuration reduces vector register utilization to 28%. To enhance performance, the register tile size was adjusted to [m,n]=[7,m4] where m4 corresponds to LMUL=4, achieving 100% utilization of vector registers.

### Recent Work

The mmt4d microkernels were implemented for the f16xf16->f32 case, with separate microkernels for LLM's prefill and decode phases.

Here are the search results for "IREE flow.dispatch region formation heuristics matmul":

## Overview of Dispatch Region Formation

Dispatch.region ops are a more lightweight abstraction for implementing fusion heuristics, i.e., the process of deciding which ops should form a dispatch region. Dispatch region formation controls fusion and partitioning decisions.

## What are Dispatch Regions?

The primary unit of interest is a "dispatch region" containing compatible computations that can be scheduled together efficiently (and safely). "Compatible" here is specified as similarly shaped workloads that indicate how many invocations a computation can be parallelized across when running in a SPMD execution model.

## Heuristics in IREE

While there are other places where heuristics are applied, the primary points for specializing the compiler are for both specific workloads and targets. Today, both of these are represented with fixed-function, C++ code based logic to imperatively inspect the program and select an appropriate pipeline.

## Matmul-Specific Considerations

Having transpose + matmul pattern in IREE might need some changes around dispatch function formation because they'd like to make them be in the same dispatch region. Additionally, dispatch regions are formed by "casting a net" around operations using a fixed heuristic that must not know about any hardware features. In particular, the introduction of packing/unpacking operations is delayed.

Links: [{"title":"Roofline x ARM: Enhancing software support for ARM SVE in MLIR and IREE","url":"https://www.roofline.ai/case-studies/sve-support"},{"title":"[CPU] Enable DT for SVE for linalg.matmul · Issue #16162 · iree-org/iree","url":"https://github.com/iree-org/iree/issues/16162"},{"title":"LLM Optimization and Deployment on SiFive RISC-V Intelligence Products","url":"https://www.sifive.com/blog/llm-optimization-and-deployment-on-sifive-intellig"},{"title":"GENERATING EFFICIENT CPU CODE WITH MLIR","url":"https://llvm.org/devmtg/2025-10/slides/quick_talks/beysel_warzynski.pdf"},{"title":"RFC: Turn the data-tiling path on by default for CPU backends","url":"https://groups.google.com/g/iree-discuss/c/lpQzYXw1r6s"},{"title":"1 The ARM Scalable Vector Extension","url":"https://alastairreid.github.io/papers/sve-ieee-micro-2017.pdf"},{"title":"[CPU][SVE] Compilation fails when scalable vectorization is enabled · Issue #16334 · iree-org/iree","url":"https://github.com/iree-org/iree/issues/16334"},{"title":"Rewriting and Optimizing Vector Length Agnostic Intrinsics from Arm SVE to RVV","url":"https://dl.acm.org/doi/fullHtml/10.1145/3677333.3678151"},{"title":"Application-oriented system modeling and optimization — Scalable System Simulations Tutorial 0.1 documentation","url":"https://csa-infra.github.io/RISCV-Scalable-Simulation-tutorial/gem5.html"},{"title":"Data tiling approach for x86 · iree-org/iree · Discussion #11821","url":"https://github.com/iree-org/iree/discussions/11821"}]

Based on the search results, here's what I found about IREE, data tiling, SVE, RVV, and scalable vectors:

## Key Findings

IREE successfully implemented an end-to-end SVE pipeline and demonstrated scalable data-tiling across a set of 13 models from different classes, solving both core challenges of enabling SVE execution and vector-length-aware tiling.

### Scalable Vectors and Data Tiling

SVE enables Vector Length Agnostic (VLA) programming, where the vector length is unknown at compile time and discovered at runtime, allowing a single binary to adapt to diverse and evolving hardware. This move towards scalable vectors mirrors a broader industry trend, with architectures like RISC-V RVV following a similar approach.

Modern ML workloads are often memory-bound, and compilers rely on data-tiling to break large tensors into small, cache-friendly blocks. In MLIR and IREE, these tiled layouts were originally designed around NEON's fixed 128-bit vector width, but with SVE's scalable vectors, these static layouts are no longer suitable: the tile shapes themselves must adapt to the vector length of the underlying CPU.

### Performance Results

Combining data-tiling with SVE delivered up to 100× speedups over non–data-tiled SVE code, with larger models seeing the strongest gains.

### Low-Precision Support and Future Work

IREE also delivered the first end-to-end support for SVE's BF16 and INT8 compute paths, establishing a solid foundation for future low-precision optimizations. The same mechanisms naturally extend to architectures such as Arm SME and RISC-V RVV, positioning this work as a blueprint for scalable vector enablement across upcoming CPU architectures.

Here are the key findings about IREE RISC-V backend compilation flags for vectorization and attributes:

## IREE Compilation Flags for RISC-V Vectorization

To enable SIMD code generation for RISC-V, you can use additional command-line flags such as `--iree-llvmcpu-target-cpu-features="+m,+a,+f,+d,+zvl512b,+v"` and `--riscv-v-fixed-length-vector-lmul-max=8`.

The flags `--riscv-v-fixed-length-vector-lmul-max` and `--riscv-v-vector-bits-min` set custom constraints for the RISC-V vector extension, such as maximum LMUL (length multiplier) and minimum vector bit width.

### Target Backend and Configuration Flags

The `--iree-hal-target-backends=llvm-cpu` specifies the target backend for compilation to the LLVM backend for CPUs, `--iree-llvm-target-triple=riscv64-unknown-elf` sets the target triple for the LLVM backend to RISC-V 64-bit architecture, `--iree-llvm-target-cpu=generic-rv64` sets the target CPU to a generic RISC-V 64-bit processor, and `--iree-llvm-target-abi=lp64d` specifies the target ABI (Application Binary Interface) for the LLVM backend.

## Vectorization Attributes and Configuration

For RISC-V function attributes, there is a `riscv_vector_cc` attribute to force the function to use the vector calling convention variant.

GCC provides `max-vectorization` and `no-max-vectorization` attributes for RISC-V functions, where `max-vectorization` tells the vectorizer to treat all vector loops as more profitable than scalar loops, and `no-max-vectorization` disables this behavior.

## IREE Architecture and Vectorization

In IREE, the input model is lowered to MLIR and then different levels of optimizations are applied (such as kernel fusion, tiling, and loop unrolling) and finally translated to target-dependent VM Bytecode.

- Looking at IREE's official documentation
- Searching for each topic individually if you want broader results

Based on the search results, here's what I found regarding IREE RISC-V vectorization, register tile size, and outer product:

## RISC-V Vectorization in IREE

IREE has been modified to enable the transformation of contraction ops into linalg.mmt4d and to perform VLEN-aware tiling for RISC-V64 target.

## Tile Size Selection

The tile sizes for the M, N, and K dimensions of input matrices are determined based on the target architecture. Choosing a smaller tile size than recommended leads to underutilization of hardware registers, while using bigger tile sizes increases register pressure that causes register spills and reloads and degrades performance.

## Register Considerations

For optimal RISC-V vectorization, implementations can be configured with parameters like mr=7 and LMUL=4, which maximizes vector register utilization. Here, LMUL (vector length multiplier) determines how many vector registers are needed to hold vector operand elements.

## Implementation Details

The transformation of linalg contraction ops to linalg.mmt4d (and tensor.pack, tensor.unpack) is performed within the iree-codegen-materialize-device-encoding pass, and once complete, the linalg.mmt4d operation gets transformed into calls to microkernel functions.

The search results provide information about IREE's RISC-V vectorization approach but don't contain specific details about outer product operations specifically. The focus is on matrix multiplication via the mmt4d (matrix multiply-transpose 4D) operation.

content.text:
Excellent! Now I have comprehensive research. Let me compile this into a detailed report for you:

## Research Summary: IREE's Matmul Code Generation for RISC-V with RVV

Based on my research, here's a comprehensive overview of how IREE handles matmul code generation for RISC-V targets with the RISC-V Vector Extension (RVV):

---

### 1. **How IREE Tiles Matmul Operations for RISC-V Targets**

IREE uses several key tiling strategies for RISC-V matmul optimization:

#### **Data Tiling and mmt4d**
- IREE transforms `linalg.matmul` operations into `linalg.mmt4d` (matrix-multiply-by-transposed-right-hand-side)
- A 4D tiled layout representation allows the compiler to express both row-major and column-major tiled layouts. For example, `tensor<2x2x2x2xf32>` represents a 4×4 matrix made of 2×2 tiles
- The transformation occurs in the `iree-codegen-materialize-device-encoding` pass

#### **Register Tiling Strategy**
- IREE implements outer product-based register tiling with output register tile size `[m, n]` where:
  - `m` = number of elements in output rows
  - `n` = number of elements in output columns
- The tile size selection is critical: undersized tiles underutilize hardware registers, while oversized tiles increase register pressure causing spills/reloads

#### **VLEN-Aware Tiling**
- IREE performs **dynamic tiling based on VLEN** (vector length) rather than fixed tiling
- Example: With VLEN=1024 and float data type with `[m,n]=[8,32]`, the initial configuration achieved only 28% vector register utilization
- Optimized configuration uses `[m,n]=[7,m4]` where m4 corresponds to LMUL=4, achieving **100% vector register utilization**

#### **RISC-V Microkernels**
- IREE has implemented mmt4d microkernels specifically for RISC-V64
- Special support for f16×f16→f32 cases with separate microkernels for LLM prefill and decode phases

---

### 2. **Compilation Flags and Attributes Affecting Vectorization**

#### **Key LLVM-CPU Target Flags**
```
--iree-hal-target-backends=llvm-cpu
--iree-llvm-target-triple=riscv64-unknown-elf
--iree-llvm-target-cpu=generic-rv64
--iree-llvm-target-abi=lp64d
```

#### **RVV-Specific Compilation Flags**
- `--iree-llvmcpu-target-cpu-features="+m,+a,+f,+d,+zvl512b,+v"` - Enables specific ISA extensions including RVV
- `--riscv-v-vector-bits-min` - Sets minimum vector bit width
- `--riscv-v-fixed-length-vector-lmul-max=8` - Sets maximum LMUL (Length Multiplier) for fixed-length vectors

#### **Vectorization Attributes**
- `riscv_vector_cc` - Forces the function to use the vector calling convention variant
- GCC provides `max-vectorization` and `no-max-vectorization` attributes for RISC-V functions

#### **Dynamic Vector Length Configuration**
- When RVV is enabled via `iree-llvm-target-cpu-features`, the compiler generates **Vector Length Specific (VLS)** code rather than fixed-length code
- Vector length is configured via `riscv-v-vector-bits-min` and `riscv-v-fixed-length-vector-lmul-max` options

---

### 3. **IREE's Dispatch Region Formation for Matmul**

#### **What are Dispatch Regions?**
- Dispatch regions are lightweight abstractions that control which operations should be fused together and scheduled as a single computation
- The primary unit is a "dispatch region" containing compatible computations that can be scheduled together (similarly shaped workloads in SPMD execution model)

#### **Dispatch Formation for Matmul**
- Operations are identified as "root" operations - for typical matmul patterns, these are:
  - The MatMul itself
  - Sum reduction operations
- For each root op, surrounding operations that could be merged are identified and grouped
- Each group creates a separate dispatch function

#### **Heuristics and Limitations**
- Dispatch regions are formed using **fixed heuristics that do NOT know about hardware features**
- The heuristic "casts a net" around operations without hardware-specific knowledge
- Introduction of packing/unpacking operations is delayed
- For transpose + matmul patterns, IREE keeps them in separate dispatch regions (no pattern-specific fusion yet)

#### **Split-K for Extra Parallelism**
- To extract additional parallelism, the reduction dimension can be split into parallel and reduction components
- This creates two dispatch regions:
  1. First dispatch: fuses linalg operations with the first generic op
  2. Second dispatch: performs remaining reduction and fuses with consumers

---

### 4. **RISC-V-Specific Tiling Heuristics and Strategies**

#### **Scalable Vector Support (Vector Length Agnostic)**
- IREE implements **Vector Length Agnostic (VLA)** programming for RVV, similar to ARM SVE
- A single binary adapts to diverse vector lengths at runtime rather than being compiled for a fixed size
- The compiler discovers vector length at runtime

#### **Scalable Data Tiling**
- IREE rewrites `linalg.matmul` into `linalg.mmt4d` with **inner tile sizes computed from runtime vector length (vscale)**
- This mechanism naturally extends from SVE to RISC-V RVV
- Tile shapes adapt to the underlying CPU's vector length

#### **Cache and Register Tiling Hierarchy**
- **Register Tiling**: Maximizes vector register utilization by organizing data to fit within vector registers
- **Cache Tiling**: Optimizes data locality, ensuring data remains in cache hierarchy as long as possible
- Both techniques work together to minimize memory access and reduce cache misses

#### **LMUL (Length Multiplier) Optimization**
- LMUL determines how many vector registers are needed to hold vector operand elements
- IREE intelligently configures LMUL (e.g., LMUL=4) to balance register utilization and vector width
- Example: mr=7 with LMUL=4 provides optimal register usage for many workloads

#### **Performance Impact**
- Through compiler optimizations on RVV, matmul-f32 performance achieved **3.1× improvement**
- Scalable data-tiling with proper vector-length awareness delivers up to **100× speedups** over non-data-tiled code

---

### 5. **Known Challenges and Ongoing Work**

#### **Compilation Time and Code Size**
- Quantized matmul vectorization on RISC-V can take **13+ minutes** to compile
- Vectorization of quantized conv1x1 ops increased library size from 215KB to >1MB
- Ongoing issue: balancing vectorization benefits against compilation overhead

#### **Active Development Areas**
- Proper distribution-tiling for mmt4d operations
- Enabling SVE data-tiling for linalg.matmul (with plans to extend to RVV)
- Optimizing low-precision (BF16, INT8) RVV support
- Split-K support for better parallelism

---

### Key Research Sources

- [Accelerating GenAI Workloads by Enabling RISC-V Microkernel Support in IREE](https://arxiv.org/html/2508.14899v1)
- [LLM Optimization and Deployment on SiFive RISC-V Intelligence Products](https://www.sifive.com/blog/llm-optimization-and-deployment-on-sifive-intellig)
- [IREE GitHub Issue: Bad quantized model compilation time for RISC-V target](https://github.com/iree-org/iree/issues/8431)
- [IREE GitHub Issue: Need to reduce device code size for RISC-V quantized vectorized workload](https://github.com/iree-org/iree/issues/8849)
- [Roofline x ARM: Enhancing software support for ARM SVE in MLIR and IREE](https://www.roofline.ai/case-studies/sve-support)
- [IREE Data-Tiling Walkthrough](https://iree.dev/community/blog/2025-08-25-data-tiling-walkthrough/)
- [IREE RISC-V Cross-Compilation Guide](https://iree.dev/building-from-source/riscv/)
- [IREE Flow Dialect Reference](https://iree.dev/reference/mlir-dialects/Flow/)
- [LLVM RISC-V Vector Extension Documentation](https://llvm.org/docs/RISCV/RISCVVectorExtension.html)
- [RFC: Turn the data-tiling path on by default for CPU backends](https://groups.google.com/g/iree-discuss/c/lpQzYXw1r6s)

---

This research provides a comprehensive understanding of IREE's sophisticated approach to RISC-V matmul optimization, emphasizing runtime vector-length awareness, scalable tiling, and careful register management for maximum performance on vector extension hardware.
